headline: ShinyHunters mass-exploited an Oracle PeopleSoft zero-day to loot hundreds of organizations while the U.S. government forced Anthropic to pull its newest AI models offline worldwide.

emerging_trends.subject: AI Weaponization
emerging_trends.text: Attackers now lean on AI for quality over volume, weaponizing Gemini for smishing and upgrading phishing lures even as raw phishing counts fall 20%.

emerging_trends.subject: Supply Chain
emerging_trends.text: Poisoned Arch AUR packages, npm 12's move to block dependency scripts, and dark-web sales of repo access all show the software pipeline as the soft underbelly.

emerging_trends.subject: Decade-Old Flaws
emerging_trends.text: A 10-year Velvet Ant authentication backdoor and a decade-old phpBB auth bypass underscore how long footholds and bugs survive unnoticed.

emerging_trends.subject: Enforcement Pressure
emerging_trends.text: Europol's AudiA6 takedown, INTERPOL's Sniper Dz arrests, a Conti guilty plea, and Google's Gemini lawsuit mark a coordinated squeeze on cybercrime infrastructure.

emerging_trends.subject: Breach Penalties
emerging_trends.text: Coupang's record $409 million fine and the $47 million 23andMe settlement signal that breach mishandling now carries steep, escalating financial cost.

topics.title: Oracle PeopleSoft Zero-Day Mass Exploitation
topics.last_24h: CISA added CVE-2026-35273 to its known exploited vulnerabilities catalog on June 12, and Google's Mandiant confirmed ShinyHunters looted hundreds of organizations.
topics.summary: ShinyHunters, which Mandiant tracks as UNC6240, exploited a missing-authentication zero-day in Oracle PeopleSoft Enterprise PeopleTools to steal gigabytes of data between May 27 and June 9, 2026. American universities took the brunt of the campaign. Oracle mitigated the flaw and published its advisory June 10, days after the theft began. Apply Oracle's fix at once and hunt systems for signs of data exfiltration.

sources.title: PeopleSoft 0-day affecting hundreds of organizations steals gigabytes of data
ShinyHunters Exploits Oracle PeopleSoft Zero-Day (CVE-2026-35273) to Breach Universities
Google Confirms Exploitation of Oracle PeopleSoft Zero-Day by ShinyHunters
CISA Adds One Known Exploited Vulnerability to Catalog

topics.title: Patch Now: Ivanti, Splunk, Check Point, Chrome
topics.last_24h: CISA's new directive BOD 26-04 gave agencies three days to patch an actively exploited Ivanti Sentry flaw as Splunk, Check Point, and Chrome shipped fixes.
topics.summary: Splunk patched CVE-2026-20253, a 9.8-severity bug that lets unauthenticated attackers run code on Splunk Enterprise below versions 10.2.4 and 10.0.7. Honeypots logged exploitation of a root-level Ivanti Sentry command-injection flaw, and WatchTowr published a proof of concept for the actively exploited Check Point VPN authentication bypass CVE-2026-50751, patched June 8. Chrome 149 closed 28 flaws, including a dozen use-after-free bugs. Patch all four immediately.

sources.title: CISA orders feds to patch actively exploited Ivanti flaw by Sunday
Critical Splunk Enterprise Flaw Lets Attackers Run Code Without Authentication
Researchers release details, PoC for exploited Check Point VPN flaw (CVE-2026-50751)
Chrome 149 Update Patches 28 Vulnerabilities

topics.title: Arch Linux AUR Supply-Chain Compromise
topics.last_24h: Attackers hijacked more than 400 Arch User Repository packages this week, rewriting build scripts to drop a Rust infostealer and an eBPF rootkit.
topics.summary: The poisoned AUR packages install a Rust credential stealer on any machine that builds them, harvesting developer secrets and access tokens. With root, the malware loads an eBPF rootkit to hide itself. The AUR is Arch Linux's community-maintained collection, separate from official repositories, so users vet packages themselves. Rebuild affected systems and rotate every exposed credential.

sources.title: Over 400 Arch Linux AUR Packages Hijacked to Deploy Infostealer and eBPF Rootkit
Over 400 Arch Linux packages compromised to push rootkit, infostealer

topics.title: U.S. Forces Anthropic to Pull Fable 5 and Mythos 5
topics.last_24h: Anthropic abruptly disabled Fable 5 and Mythos 5 globally after a 5:21 p.m. ET federal order on June 12 barred foreign-national access.
topics.summary: The Trump administration ordered Anthropic to block all foreign nationals from its most advanced models, inside or outside the United States, citing national security and new export controls. Anthropic complied by suspending both models worldwide yet disputed the rationale, calling a cited Fable 5 jailbreak narrow and the underlying capability common elsewhere. The order stranded paying customers and set a precedent for direct government control over frontier AI access.

sources.title: US Gov asks Anthropic to ban 'foreign national' access to Fable, Mythos
U.S. Orders Anthropic to Suspend Fable 5 and Mythos 5 Access for Foreign Nationals
Anthropic Says It Has Taken Its Latest AI Models Offline to Comply With New Export Controls
Anthropic Disputes Fable 5 AI Jailbreak

topics.title: AI Coding Agents Become an Attack Surface
topics.last_24h: Researchers disclosed Agentjacking against AI coding agents and a LangGraph flaw chain that enables remote code execution.
topics.summary: Tenet Security described Agentjacking, which plants a fake Sentry error report to trick AI coding agents into running arbitrary code on developer machines. Researchers separately found three flaws in LangChain's LangGraph framework, including an SQL injection that chains into remote code execution on self-hosted AI agents. Both show how autonomous agents widen the developer attack surface. Patch LangGraph and constrain what coding agents may execute.

sources.title: Agentjacking Attack Tricks AI Coding Agents Into Running Malicious Code
LangGraph Flaw Chain Exposes Self-Hosted AI Agents to Remote Code Execution

topics.title: Velvet Ant Decade-Long Linux Auth Backdoor
topics.last_24h: Sygnia detailed how the China-nexus group Velvet Ant backdoored Linux PAM and OpenSSH to lurk inside an isolated network for nearly a decade.
topics.summary: Velvet Ant seized a victim's authentication stack, planting backdoors in the PAM and OpenSSH components that decide who signs in, which gave it full visibility into administrative activity for roughly 10 years. The target ran an isolated network with no internet access, and the group still held persistence beyond the reach of ordinary cleanup. Defenders should audit login-path binaries and authentication modules for tampering.

sources.title: Chinese hackers hijack auth flow, spy on isolated network for a decade
China-Linked Hackers Backdoored Linux Login Software to Hide for Nearly a Decade

topics.title: Coordinated Cybercrime Crackdown
topics.last_24h: Europol dismantled the AudiA6 laundering service, a Ukrainian pleaded guilty over Conti, and Google sued a China-based Gemini-abusing smishing network.
topics.summary: Europol took down AudiA6, which washed more than €336 million in ransomware and other criminal proceeds since 2022. A Ukrainian national extradited from Ireland pleaded guilty to conspiracy in the Conti ransomware operation, and INTERPOL's Operation Ramz disrupted the decade-old Sniper Dz phishing-as-a-service platform with 201 arrests across 13 MENA countries. Google sued Outsider Enterprise for weaponizing its Gemini AI to run phishing that hit hundreds of thousands of victims.

sources.title: Europol Disrupts AudiA6 Crypto Laundering Service Used by Ransomware Gangs
Ukrainian national pleads guilty to role in Conti ransomware operation
INTERPOL Operation Takes Down Sniper Dz Phishing Platform, Arrests Administrator
Google Sues Chinese Smishing Network Accused of Using Gemini AI in Phishing

topics.title: Record Breach Fines and Major Disclosures
topics.last_24h: South Korea fined Coupang a record $409 million, a court approved a $47 million 23andMe settlement, and Novo Nordisk disclosed a clinical-trials breach.
topics.summary: South Korea's commission hit Coupang with its largest-ever data-breach penalty, $409 million, eclipsing this year's SK Telecom fine. A bankruptcy administrator approved a $47 million fund for roughly 7 million 23andMe customers whose genetic data hackers stole starting April 2023. Novo Nordisk, the world's largest insulin maker, reported a breach of some clinical-trial patient data, and France said a Tchap messenger breach exposed over 73,000 public-sector accounts.

sources.title: South Korea hits Coupang with record $409 million fine over data breach
Bankruptcy admin approves settlement fund of $47 million for 23andMe data breach victims
Pharma giant Novo Nordisk discloses breach of clinical trials data
Over 73,000 French govt employees affected in Tchap messenger breach

business_politics.text: Trump says the United States and Iran will sign a deal Sunday to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, capping a week that swung from a helicopter-crash escalation back to a ceasefire; oil futures settled at multimonth lows as the prospect of reopening firmed.

sources.title: Trump says US and Iran will sign deal on Sunday to reopen Strait
Oil Futures Fall on Expectations of U.S.-Iran Agreement

business_politics.text: U.S. headline inflation reached 4.2% annually, the highest since April 2023, sharpening the Federal Reserve's choice between waiting and easing.

sources.title: Chart of the Week: US headline inflation matters to consumers

business_politics.text: SpaceX raised $75 billion in the largest initial public offering on record, its shares jumping nearly a fifth to value the company at $2.1 trillion and making Elon Musk the world's first trillionaire.

sources.title: SpaceX's surge on debut makes Musk world's first trillionaire

business_politics.text: The Justice Department cleared Paramount's $81 billion acquisition of Warner Bros. Discovery, advancing David Ellison's media empire; the deal still needs European approval.

sources.title: Justice Department Clears Paramount-Warner Bros. Discovery Deal

business.text: The Penguins acquired the rights to Oliver Okuliar, a 26-year-old unsigned winger coming off 29 points in Sweden's top league, from Florida for defenseman Emil Pieniniemi.

around_town.text: A $6.8 million Stewart Avenue project will rebuild stormwater infrastructure to curb localized flooding in two Pittsburgh neighborhoods.

sources.title: $6.8M project aims to reduce flooding in 2 Pittsburgh neighborhoods

around_town.text: Pennsylvania American Water lifted the boil-water advisory covering about 1,500 customers in Clairton, in place since June 7 after a temporary loss of pressure.

around_town.text: Part of the Great Allegheny Passage in West Mifflin will close the evening of July 9 while contractors scale rockfall hazards along the trail.

around_town.text: State troopers issued nearly 700 citations in the first week enforcing Paul Miller's Law, which bans handheld phone use while driving in Pennsylvania.

sources.title: During first week of Paul Miller's Law enforcement, troopers issue nearly 700 citations

reading.author: Ed Zitron
reading.title: Premium: The Silicon Valley Bubble (Part 1)
reading.summary: Zitron argues the AI era is ending as OpenAI and Anthropic both file to go public, racing for exit liquidity while burning billions a year with no path to profit.

reading.author: Stratechery
reading.title: An Interview with Ben Bajarin About Apple, AI, and Compute
reading.summary: Ben Thompson and Ben Bajarin dissect Apple's WWDC, the long-delayed arrival of on-device Intelligence, and the shifting economics of the AI compute industry.

reading.author: Cal Newport
reading.title: Why Isn't AI Taking Our Jobs?
reading.summary: Newport questions the AI industry's favorite analogy to industrial automation, examining why the technology has yet to eliminate work the way machines once displaced manual labor.
